questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes presents with sudden onset of right-sided weakness and inability to speak. On examination, he has right-sided hemiparesis, dense right facial droop, and global aphasia. BP 160/90, HR 80 regular. CT head shows no evidence of bleeding. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The acute onset of focal neurological deficits with a CT head negative for bleeding makes ischaemic stroke the most likely. Haemorrhagic stroke would show hyperdensity on CT. Subdural haematoma usually has a history of trauma and may be subacute. Brain tumour typically has progressive symptoms. Encephalitis often presents with fever, altered mental status, and seizures.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes experiences a 15-minute episode of transient left arm weakness and difficulty finding words. He is now completely back to normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The transient nature (<24 hours) and rapid resolution of focal neurological deficits are typical of a TIA. Ischaemic stroke would have persistent symptoms. Migraine aura usually includes headache and visual symptoms. Hypoglycaemia can cause focal symptoms but is usually accompanied by autonomic and cognitive changes. Functional disorder often has inconsistent features.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with hypertension is found unconscious. On examination, eyes deviated to the left, dense right hemiplegia, and aphasia. CT head shows early subtle loss of grey-white differentiation in the left MCA territory and no hyperdensity. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The acute onset and CT showing early ischaemic changes without blood point to ischaemic stroke. Haemorrhagic stroke would show hyperdense blood. Subarachnoid haemorrhage would show blood in the cisterns. Brainstem stroke would cause crossed signs. Encephalitis usually presents with fever and altered consciousness.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with a known left MCA ischaemic stroke 3 days ago now develops sudden worsening of his right-sided weakness and a new headache. CT head shows a large intraparenchymal haemorrhage within the same territory. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In a patient with recent ischaemic stroke, new haemorrhage in the same territory is typical of haemorrhagic transformation, often related to reperfusion or anticoagulation. Recurrent ischaemic stroke would not cause acute haemorrhage. Subdural haematoma is outside the parenchyma. Cerebral oedema would cause midline shift without blood. Septic emboli would show multiple lesions.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman, smoker and on combined oral contraceptive pill, presents with sudden onset of left arm and leg weakness and left facial droop. She has no headache. CT head is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Sudden onset of focal neurological deficits in a young woman with risk factors (smoking, OCP) is consistent with ischaemic stroke. Haemorrhagic stroke would likely cause headache and show blood on CT. Migraine aura typically includes positive visual symptoms and evolves over minutes. Multiple sclerosis often has relapsing-remitting episodes and different MRI findings. Conversion disorder has inconsistent physical signs.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 20-minute episode of right arm weakness and slurred speech that resolved completely. She has no past medical history. CT head is normal. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm a diagnosis of transient ischaemic attack (TIA)?
options: CT angiography of head and neck
questions.explanation: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is the gold standard to detect acute infarction in TIA, as it can show evidence of ischaemia even when symptoms have resolved. Carotid ultrasound and CT angiography are useful for vascular imaging but not to confirm the TIA diagnosis itself. ECG detects arrhythmias, and TOE is for cardiac source, neither confirms the TIA.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes presents with acute onset left hemiparesis and neglect 6 hours ago. His NIHSS score is 8. Non-contrast CT head is normal. What is the single most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis of ischaemic stroke?
options: CT angiography of head and neck
questions.explanation: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is the most sensitive and specific test to confirm acute ischaemic stroke, especially within the first few hours. CT perfusion may show penumbra but is not diagnostic of infarction. CT angiography evaluates vessels, not parenchyma. Carotid ultrasound assesses stenoses, and repeat CT would miss early ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with acute onset right hemiplegia and global aphasia 3 hours ago. His blood pressure is 180/100 mmHg. Non-contrast CT head shows a hyperdense left middle cerebral artery sign but no haemorrhage. Which investigation should be performed urgently to assess for large vessel occlusion amenable to thrombectomy?
options: CT angiography of head and neck
questions.explanation: CT angiography (CTA) rapidly identifies large vessel occlusion (e.g., MCA, ICA) and is the gold standard for selecting thrombectomy candidates. MRI DWI confirms infarction but delays treatment. CT perfusion shows penumbra but does not definitively show occlusion. Transcranial Doppler is operator-dependent, and DSA is invasive and not first-line.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with a known ischaemic stroke affecting the left hemisphere 3 days ago suddenly deteriorates with new headache, vomiting, and reduced consciousness. Her blood pressure is 200/110 mmHg. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the suspected complication?
questions.explanation: Repeat non-contrast CT head is the first-line test to detect haemorrhagic transformation of ischaemic stroke, which presents with acute deterioration. It is rapid and sensitive for acute blood. MRI GRE is also sensitive but slower and less available. CTA is for vascular lesions, LP is for subarachnoid haemorrhage, and venogram for venous thrombosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with no cardiovascular risk factors presents with acute onset right homonymous hemianopia and left facial droop. CT head is normal. She has a history of migraine with aura. Twelve-lead ECG shows sinus rhythm. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm a potential cardioembolic source such as patent foramen ovale?
options: MR angiography of the circle of Willis
Transthoracic echocardiogram with agitated saline bubble study
questions.explanation: Transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE) is the gold standard for detecting patent foramen ovale (PFO) and other cardiac sources of embolism, especially in cryptogenic stroke. TTE with bubble study is less sensitive and may miss small shunts. Carotid duplex and MRA assess arterial causes, CT venogram evaluates cerebral veins.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old right-handed man presents with acute onset of mild left-sided weakness and facial droop (NIHSS 4) that started 2 hours ago. CT head shows no haemorrhage, and there are no contraindications to thrombolysis. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Alteplase (thrombolysis) is indicated for acute ischaemic stroke within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, even in mild deficits, provided no contraindications. Aspirin is given after 24 hours if no thrombolysis. Mechanical thrombectomy is reserved for large vessel occlusion.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with acute right-sided hemiparesis and global aphasia (NIHSS 18) 5 hours after symptom onset. CT angiogram shows a proximal left MCA occlusion. CT head shows no haemorrhage. She has no contraindications to intervention. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Mechanical thrombectomy is first-line for acute ischaemic stroke with confirmed large vessel occlusion within 6 hours of onset. Alteplase is not given because >4.5 hours. Aspirin is not acute treatment. Hemicraniectomy is for malignant oedema later.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a large left MCA ischaemic stroke 48 hours ago develops a decreasing GCS from 14 to 9 over 6 hours. CT head shows early signs of malignant MCA syndrome with significant midline shift. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Decompressive hemicraniectomy is the definitive treatment for malignant MCA syndrome (life-threatening cerebral oedema). Mannitol and hyperventilation are temporary measures. Thrombolysis is too late. Hypothermia is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with known paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents with acute-onset dysarthria and right arm weakness (NIHSS 3) 3 hours ago. CT head excludes haemorrhage, and she is thrombolysed. Now 3 weeks later, she is stable with no neurological deficits. She has a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 5. What is the single most appropriate treatment for long-term stroke prevention?
questions.explanation: For AF-related stroke, a DOAC (apixaban) is first-line for long-term anticoagulation. Warfarin is an alternative but not first-line. Antiplatelets are inferior. Starting at 3 weeks post minor stroke is appropriate per NICE.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents with a lacunar ischaemic stroke (NIHSS 3) confirmed on MRI. He has no atrial fibrillation, no large vessel disease, and is haemodynamically stable. He is currently taking aspirin 75 mg daily. What is the single most appropriate first-line antiplatelet for long-term secondary prevention?
options: Add dipyridamole MR 200 mg twice daily to aspirin
Switch to ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily
questions.explanation: NICE recommends clopidogrel monotherapy as first-line for secondary prevention after ischaemic stroke or TIA in sinus rhythm. Aspirin alone is inferior; aspirin-plus-dipyridamole is second-line. Ticagrelor is not indicated in this setting. Warfarin is for cardioembolic stroke.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with sudden onset of right-sided weakness and slurred speech. Symptoms started 1.5 hours ago. He has no known comorbidities and is not on any anticoagulation. His blood pressure is 155/90 mmHg, and his National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) score is 6. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request a CT angiogram of the head and neck
Perform an urgent non-contrast CT scan of the head
questions.explanation: Before thrombolysis can be considered, a non-contrast CT head is essential to exclude haemorrhagic stroke, which would contraindicate alteplase. This is the priority over any treatment or further imaging.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation on warfarin presents with acute onset of dense right hemiplegia and global aphasia. She was last seen well 2 hours ago. Her INR is 2.8. An urgent non-contrast CT head shows no haemorrhage but subtle early signs of a left middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory infarct. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform an urgent CT angiogram of the head and neck
Reverse warfarin with prothrombin complex concentrate
questions.explanation: Thrombolysis is contraindicated due to elevated INR (>1.7). For a large vessel occlusion within 6 hours, endovascular thrombectomy is beneficial. CT angiography is needed to assess for proximal MCA occlusion, guiding thrombectomy candidacy. Reversing warfarin is not indicated unless haemorrhage occurs.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man received intravenous alteplase for a left MCA ischaemic stroke 2 hours ago. He now develops severe headache, vomiting, and a decreasing level of consciousness. His blood pressure is 190/100 mmHg. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform an emergency non-contrast CT scan of the head
Place the patient in a head-down tilt
questions.explanation: Post-thrombolysis neurological deterioration with headache raises suspicion of haemorrhagic transformation. Immediate non-contrast CT head is mandatory to confirm or exclude intracranial haemorrhage, as this would alter management (e.g., stop any antithrombotic, consider reversal).

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with a 20-minute episode of left arm weakness and slurred speech that has fully resolved. Her ABCD2 score is 4. An urgent CT head is normal. She has known paroxysmal atrial fibrillation but is not on anticoagulation. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: For a TIA with atrial fibrillation, NICE recommends starting a direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban) once intracranial haemorrhage is excluded. Antiplatelets are less effective in cardioembolism. Further tests like TOE may be considered but are not the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman who is 20 weeks pregnant presents with acute onset of left-sided weakness and dysarthria. She was last seen well 1 hour ago. Her blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg, glucose normal. An urgent non-contrast CT head shows no haemorrhage. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a CT angiogram of the head and neck
Perform an MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging
questions.explanation: Pregnancy is not an absolute contraindication to IV thrombolysis; the potential benefit of alteplase in a young patient with disabling stroke within 4.5 hours outweighs the risks. MRI would delay treatment, and other options are not first-line after excluding haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to the Emergency Department with acute-onset slurred speech and right facial droop. His capillary blood glucose is 2.8 mmol/L. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his neurological symptoms?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycaemia can perfectly mimic stroke. Blood glucose below 3.5 mmol/L in a diabetic patient should be corrected before stroke diagnosis is confirmed.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman is admitted with dense left hemiparesis and neglect. CT brain performed 1.5 hours after onset shows subtle loss of grey-white differentiation in the right MCA territory. Six hours later she becomes drowsy (GCS 12). Which clinical finding is most concerning for malignant MCA infarction?
options: Pupillary asymmetry (right pupil 5 mm, left 3 mm)
questions.explanation: Pupillary asymmetry indicates uncal herniation from massive oedema. This is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate escalation.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known atrial fibrillation is admitted with a left MCA ischaemic stroke. He is not on anticoagulation. On day 2, he develops sudden severe headache, vomiting, and a fall in GCS from 15 to 11. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Haemorrhagic transformation is a common complication of large cardioembolic strokes, typically presenting with acute worsening, headache, vomiting, and reduced consciousness.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old right-handed man presents with expressive aphasia and right arm weakness that started 3 hours ago. NIHSS score is 10. Non-contrast CT brain is normal. Which imaging finding on the CT scan would most strongly suggest a proximal large vessel occlusion?
options: Old lacunar infarct in left thalamus
questions.explanation: Hyperdense MCA sign represents intraluminal thrombus in the proximal MCA. It is highly specific for large vessel occlusion and should prompt urgent CTA.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of vertigo, left-sided ataxia, and dysphagia. She reports having had a chiropractic neck manipulation the previous day. Which single finding in her history is most concerning for a specific stroke aetiology?
questions.explanation: Neck manipulation is a known cause of vertebral artery dissection, which can present with posterior circulation stroke symptoms. This requires urgent imaging (CTA/MRA) and consideration of anticoagulation.
